title: Pressure Effects on CeMnSi: Evolution of Ce 4f and Mn 3d Electronic States and Negative Thermal Expansion
authors: Sae Nishiyama, Haruka Mima, Jun-ichi Hayashi, Keiki Takeda, Chihiro Sekine, Yoshiya Uwatoko, Hiroki Takahashi, Hiroshi Tanida, Yukihiro Kawamura
abstract: We investigated pressure effects on the nontrivial heavy-fermion antiferromagnet CeMnSi by means of electrical resistivity and powder X-ray diffraction. With increasing pressure, the antiferromagnetic order of Mn (T_N ~ 240 K at ambient pressure) is rapidly suppressed and disappears at P_c ~ 1.3 GPa. Instead, a pressure-induced anomaly appears at T_M ~ 97 K and shifts to higher temperatures with increasing pressure. The switching of the Mn magnetic state may reflect a modification of the magnetic symmetry of the system, which could influence the stability of the heavy-fermion state. In the low-pressure region, non-Fermi-liquid-like behavior characterized by nearly T-linear resistivity is observed around 0.7 GPa. In addition, the resistivity shows a marked reduction below T_M and a qualitative change toward more metallic behavior above the structural transition pressure P_s ~ 5.7 GPa. At ambient pressure, CeMnSi exhibits negative thermal expansion below ~40 K, which is absent in LaMnSi, supporting the formation of a heavy-fermion ground state.
summary: This study demonstrates how applied pressure can tune the magnetic and structural phases of the heavy-fermion material CeMnSi. It reveals that suppressing Mn-3d magnetism leads to non-Fermi-liquid behavior and that a structural transition at high pressure modifies the electronic state. The findings highlight the strong coupling between Ce-4f electrons and the crystal lattice.
main_problem: Investigating the pressure-induced evolution of electronic, magnetic, and structural properties in the heavy-fermion antiferromagnet CeMnSi, specifically the interplay between Ce-4f and Mn-3d electrons.
main_result: Pressure suppresses Mn antiferromagnetic order at 1.3 GPa, inducing non-Fermi-liquid behavior and a new magnetic anomaly, followed by a structural transition to a monoclinic phase at 5.7 GPa.
method: High-pressure electrical resistivity measurements using piston-cylinder and cubic-anvil apparatus, and synchrotron powder X-ray diffraction.
model_or_system: The physical system is CeMnSi, a heavy-fermion antiferromagnet with a tetragonal CeFeSi-type structure, compared against the non-4f reference compound LaMnSi.
key_observables: Néel temperature (TN), pressure-induced anomaly temperature (TM), structural transition pressure (Ps), resistivity exponent (n), and lattice constants (a, c).
important_parameters: Critical pressures Pc ~ 1.3 GPa and Ps ~ 5.7 GPa; temperature scales for Mn-AFM order and heavy-fermion formation.
main_assumptions: The structural transition is driven by lattice instabilities associated with Ce-4f electrons rather than pure valence instability.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Crystal structure; Fig 2: Temperature dependence of resistivity under pressure; Fig 3: Resistivity power-law fits and exponent n; Fig 4: XRD patterns and lattice constants; Fig 5: High-pressure XRD patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CeMnSi system, details the experimental pressure/temperature setup, presents transport and structural results showing magnetic and structural transitions, and concludes with a discussion on the 3d-4f hybridization mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (Color online) Crystal structure of CeMnSi with its lay- ered arrangement, drawn using the VESTA software.7) (a) Primi- tive unit cell; (b) and (c) views along the a and c axes, respectively. The tetragonal CeFeSi-type structure consists of alternating Ce– Ce layers and Mn–Si layers stacked along the c axis.

Fig. 2. (Color online) (a) Temperature dependence of ρ of CeMnSi under various pressures. (b) Temperature dependence of dρ/dT. For clarity, the dρ/dT curves are vertically offset. The ar- rows indicate characteristic temperatures and are guides to the eye (see text).

Fig. 3. (Color online)(a) Low-temperature ρ of CeMnSi plotted as a function of T 2 at representative pressures. (b) ρ plotted as a function of T at the same pressures; dashed lines in (a) and (b) are guides to the T 2 and T dependences, respectively. (c) Plot of ln(ρ −ρ0) versus ln T; the solid line is a linear fit according to ln(ρ −ρ0) = ln A + n ln T. (d) Pressure dependence of the exponent n. (e) Pressure dependence of the residual resistivity ρ0 and resistivity at 1.5 K, ρ1.5K of CeMnSi.

Fig. 4. (Color online) Temperature-dependent XRD powder patterns of (a) CeMnSi and (b) LaMnSi measured at 1 atm. The calculated diffraction patterns for the P4/nmm space group are also shown. Asterisks indicate diffraction peaks originating from the Mylar window of the GM refrigerator and Apiezon grease. Temperature dependences of (c) the lattice constant a, (d) the lattice constant c, and (e) the unit-cell volume V for CeMnSi and LaMnSi at 1 atm.

Fig. 6. (Color online) Temperature–pressure phase diagram of CeMnSi constructed from the powder XRD measurements. The low-pressure tetragonal phase (circles) and the high-pressure mon- oclinic phase (squares) are separated by a first-order structural transition, accompanied by a two-phase coexistence region (trian- gles) near Ps. The dashed line is a guide to the eye.
